message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T111 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T111    Base commit: 356d850 (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard 356d850` first; confirm docs/install.md has the §4 NM section (T110) and the tun_persist reference (T109) before starting.
headline: Ship the templated wanbond-addressing oneshot unit + C4 persistence recipe (Q40 artifact + C4)
description: Add packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service: a templated oneshot (instance = role) with `PartOf=wanbond-%i.service`, `After=wanbond-%i.service`, that re-applies address + link-up + policy rules + per-table routes + nft SNAT from an operator-owned environment/script file after the daemon (re)starts. It MUST NOT race tun creation (the R27 lesson: a plain ExecStartPost under Type=exec runs before wanbond0 exists) — wait for the interface (ExecStartPre poll loop or BindsTo/After=sys-subsystem-net-devices-wanbond0.device). Coupled C4 doc section in install.md: the persistence recipe for non-networkd hosts, blessing this unit, explicitly warning that a plain ExecStartPost races tun creation, and noting the oneshot becomes optional-but-harmless once tun_persist is enabled.
acceptance: systemd-analyze verify passes on the unit (where available in CI/dev shell); the unit orders after interface existence, not just after execve (documented rationale referencing the R27 race); install.md C4 section references the shipped file and carries the race warning + the tun_persist cross-link. go test ./... unaffected.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...`. If `systemd-analyze` is available in the nix dev shell, run `systemd-analyze verify packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service` and include its output; if NOT available, note that and validate the unit by careful inspection against systemd.unit(5)/systemd.service(5) syntax. Look at any existing packaging/systemd/ units to match style; if none exist, follow standard templated-unit conventions. Add a Go presence/shape test for the shipped file if the repo has a packaging_test.go pattern (T110 added internal/config/packaging_test.go — extend or mirror it to assert the unit exists + carries the interface-existence ordering, so the artifact is CI-guarded).
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t111-fix` off 356d850 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.

- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. - dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
600	  concentrator must set neither. Entries are `ip:port` literals only (no DNS),
601	  de-duplicated.
602	- **Distinct `source_addr`.** Each `[[paths]]` needs a unique, valid
603	  `source_addr` (compared unmapped, so `192.0.2.10` and `::ffff:192.0.2.10`
604	  collide). `name` must also be unique.
605	- **`allowed_ips`.** At least one CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG
606	  configuration is assembled (not by `config.validate`).
607	- **amnezia all-or-nothing.** An unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain
608	  WireGuard. Once *any* of `jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4` is set, the full
609	  `jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2` set must be `> 0`, `jmin <= jmax`, the init/response
610	  lengths must not collide (`148+s1 != 92+s2`), and `h1..h4` must be a distinct
611	  set (omitted headers default to `1..4`). The same profile must be set on both
612	  ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
613	- **`link_bandwidth` + `link_rtt` are a pair.** Both-or-neither, and
614	  **all-or-nothing across every path**: declare on every `[[paths]]` block or
615	  none (the shared pace is sized to the slowest declared link, undefined under a
616	  partial declaration). They take effect **only** under
617	  `scheduler.policy = "weighted"` with `pacing_enabled = true`; otherwise a
618	  declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are **mutually exclusive** with
619	  the raw `scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps` / `pacing_burst_frames` knobs —
620	  declare link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot knobs, not both.
621	- **scheduler off-unless-present.** No `[scheduler]` block ⇒ `active-backup` and
622	  every weighted knob ignored. Under `weighted`, omitted knobs take the defaults
623	  shown; the hysteresis band requires `disengage_fraction < engage_fraction`.
624	- **fec off-unless-present.** No `[fec]` block (or `enabled = false`) ⇒ no
625	  parity on the wire. `adaptive = true` requires `enabled = true`. In adaptive
626	  mode set **either** `target_residual` (recommended, `(0,1)`) **or**
627	  `safety_factor` (`>= 1`), never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
628	- **metrics loopback-only.** `[metrics] listen` must be a loopback address (or a
629	  hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
630	  the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.
631	
632	## 4. systemd units
633	
634	Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:
635	
636	```sh
637	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
638	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
639	systemctl daemon-reload
640	systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
641	```
642

647	  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
648	- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
649	  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
650	  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.

653	
654	On systems running **NetworkManager** (the default on RPi OS, Debian, and Ubuntu
655	desktop), NetworkManager monitors the `wanbond0` interface for link changes and
656	automatically flushes any configured IP addresses on link-up, destroying the
657	tunnel routing without operator action. This failure mode (D39, D5) is prevented
658	by marking `wanbond0` as an unmanaged device in NetworkManager's configuration.
659	
660	Deploy the shipped drop-in:
661	
662	```sh
663	cp packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/
664	```

670	# or
671	sudo systemctl reload NetworkManager
672	```

675	
676	```sh

678	```
679

681	
682	If your edge box runs **systemd-networkd** instead, and NetworkManager is **not running**,
683	you do not need this drop-in — skip this subsection and proceed to "Interface
684	addressing and routing" below. (If NetworkManager is active alongside systemd-networkd,
685	the drop-in is still required to prevent the address flush.)
686	
687	### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)
688	
689	The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
690	administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
691	so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
692	but owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses or routes**
693	(wg-quick style, no privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a
694	**systemd-networkd `.network` file**, using the inner addresses from
695	`allowed_ips`:
696	
697	```ini

699	[Match]
700	Name=wanbond0
701	
702	[Network]
703	Address=10.77.0.2/24
704	```
705	
706	```sh
707	systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
708	```
709

711	drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
712	the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
713	and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
714	with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
715	unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
716	watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
717	appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
718	there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)
719	
720	#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)
721	
722	By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
723	the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
724	it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
725	dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
726	any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.
727	
728	Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
729	issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
730	stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
731	device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
732	referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to
733	`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to
734	being torn down on stop.

737	> NetworkManager `unmanaged-devices` drop-in on NM-managed hosts. Persistence
738	> keeps the link alive across restarts; it does **not** exempt the interface
739	> from NetworkManager, which would otherwise flush the addresses/routes the
740	> operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of
741	> `tun_persist`.

744	
745	### Concentrator: UDP listen port
746	
747	Allow the WireGuard UDP port in from the WAN (and, on OCI, in the subnet's
748	security list / NSG as well — both layers must pass):
749	
750	```sh
751	iptables -I INPUT -p udp --dport 51820 -j ACCEPT
752	```
753	
754	### Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT — REQUIRED (OCI default-REJECT caveat)
755	
756	The concentrator **must ACCEPT traffic arriving on the wanbond tunnel
757	interface, ordered ahead of any default REJECT rule**:
758	
759	```sh
760	iptables -I INPUT -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
761	```
762	
763	Why this is required: Oracle Cloud (OCI) images ship an INPUT chain ending in
764	
765	```

767	```
768

770	earlier rule, so **ping through the tunnel works while any TCP connection
771	through the tunnel fails with a confusing `No route to host`** (the REJECT's
772	ICMP host-prohibited answer). This exact failure was hit during P0 real-host
773	testing. `iptables -I` (insert at head) places the ACCEPT ahead of the appended
774	(`-A`) REJECT; verify the ordering with `iptables -S INPUT` — the tunnel ACCEPT
775	must appear before the `-j REJECT` line. The same applies to any distro or
776	provider whose default firewall ends INPUT with REJECT/DROP (e.g. firewalld
777	zones): treat "tunnel ACCEPT ahead of the default reject" as a required
778	concentrator deployment step.

concentrator must set neither. Entries are `ip:port` literals only (no DNS),
  de-duplicated.
- **Distinct `source_addr`.** Each `[[paths]]` needs a unique, valid
  `source_addr` (compared unmapped, so `192.0.2.10` and `::ffff:192.0.2.10`
  collide). `name` must also be unique.
- **`allowed_ips`.** At least one CIDR per peer; empty is rejected when the WG
  configuration is assembled (not by `config.validate`).
- **amnezia all-or-nothing.** An unconfigured (all-zero) block is plain
  WireGuard. Once *any* of `jc/jmin/jmax/s1/s2/h1..h4` is set, the full
  `jc,jmin,jmax,s1,s2` set must be `> 0`, `jmin <= jmax`, the init/response
  lengths must not collide (`148+s1 != 92+s2`), and `h1..h4` must be a distinct
  set (omitted headers default to `1..4`). The same profile must be set on both
  ends. One obfuscation engine per process.
- **`link_bandwidth` + `link_rtt` are a pair.** Both-or-neither, and
  **all-or-nothing across every path**: declare on every `[[paths]]` block or
  none (the shared pace is sized to the slowest declared link, undefined under a
  partial declaration). They take effect **only** under
  `scheduler.policy = "weighted"` with `pacing_enabled = true`; otherwise a
  declared bandwidth is inert. When active they are **mutually exclusive** with
  the raw `scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps` / `pacing_burst_frames` knobs —
  declare link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot knobs, not both.
- **scheduler off-unless-present.** No `[scheduler]` block ⇒ `active-backup` and
  every weighted knob ignored. Under `weighted`, omitted knobs take the defaults
  shown; the hysteresis band requires `disengage_fraction < engage_fraction`.
- **fec off-unless-present.** No `[fec]` block (or `enabled = false`) ⇒ no
  parity on the wire. `adaptive = true` requires `enabled = true`. In adaptive
  mode set **either** `target_residual` (recommended, `(0,1)`) **or**
  `safety_factor` (`>= 1`), never both; both must stay 0 (unset) in fixed mode.
- **metrics loopback-only.** `[metrics] listen` must be a loopback address (or a
  hostname resolving entirely to loopback); a non-loopback bind is refused when
  the endpoint starts. Omit the block to serve no metrics at all.

## 4. systemd units

Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:

```sh
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
```

- `systemctl reload wanbond-<role>` sends SIGHUP: the daemon re-reads the
  config and applies the **path diff** (add/remove uplinks) without tearing
  down the live tunnel. A bad reload is logged and the running config kept.
- `Restart=on-failure` restarts the daemon if the tunnel engine tears down
  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.

desktop), NetworkManager monitors the `wanbond0` interface for link changes and

```sh

```

Then reload NetworkManager to apply the change:

```sh

# or
sudo systemctl reload NetworkManager
```

```sh
nmcli device show wanbond0 | grep STATE
```

The output should show `STATE: unmanaged`.

If your edge box runs **systemd-networkd** instead, and NetworkManager is **not running**,
you do not need this drop-in — skip this subsection and proceed to "Interface
addressing and routing" below. (If NetworkManager is active alongside systemd-networkd,
the drop-in is still required to prevent the address flush.)

### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)

The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —

(wg-quick style, no privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a
**systemd-networkd `.network` file**, using the inner addresses from
`allowed_ips`:

```ini

Name=wanbond0

[Network]
Address=10.77.0.2/24
```

```sh
systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
```

Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`

the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —

with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)

#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)

By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.

Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to

### Concentrator: UDP listen port

Allow the WireGuard UDP port in from the WAN (and, on OCI, in the subnet's
security list / NSG as well — both layers must pass):



# wanbond edge role: the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
# Install: cp wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/ && systemctl daemon-reload
# Config:  /etc/wanbond/edge.toml, root-owned, mode 0600 EXACTLY (the daemon
#          refuses anything else — the file holds the WG private key and PSK).
# See docs/install.md for addressing/routing drop-ins and firewall requirements.

[Unit]
Description=wanbond bonded WAN tunnel (edge role)
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target
ConditionPathExists=/etc/wanbond/edge.toml

[Service]
Type=exec
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/wanbond --config /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
# SIGHUP re-reads the config and applies the path diff (add/remove uplinks)
# without tearing down the live tunnel.
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=2s

# The daemon needs only CAP_NET_ADMIN (TUN device creation); it does no
# interface addressing or routing itself (see docs/install.md).
CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN
DeviceAllow=/dev/net/tun rw
NoNewPrivileges=yes
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=yes
PrivateTmp=yes
ProtectKernelTunables=yes
ProtectKernelModules=yes
ProtectKernelLogs=yes
ProtectControlGroups=yes
ProtectClock=yes
ProtectHostname=yes
RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_NETLINK AF_UNIX
RestrictNamespaces=yes
RestrictRealtime=yes
RestrictSUIDSGID=yes
LockPersonality=yes
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=yes
SystemCallArchitectures=native
SystemCallFilter=@system-service
UMask=0077

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Note the shipped docs (T22/R27, docs/install.md §4) cover only systemd-networkd addressing — the NM case is undocumented (fixes-doc C1). Fix direction split across: daemon brings the link UP itself (fixes-doc I1, low-risk; addressing stays operator-owned), actionable EIO diagnostics (fixes-doc I3), interface/route persistence across restarts (fixes-doc I7), and the NM + oneshot doc recipes (fixes-doc C1/C4) — the improvements/doc side is tracked in the wanbond-fixes goal; this defect item covers the DOWN-link/NM failure mode itself."

ROOT CAUSE (found on hardware): bidirectional failover needs BOTH ends to independently mark the killed path DOWN via their probe-liveness Tick, which rode ONE wall-clock ticker goroutine (StartProbeLoop->emitProbes); on the concentrator (iperf3 server absorbing the forward flood on 4 vCPU) that goroutine was scheduled with ~1s jitter under GOMAXPROCS saturation, delaying the reply-direction switch past 3s in ~30% of runs. FIX: tickLivenessFromReceive — a throttled (atomic-CAS, <=once/interval) liveness sweep driven off the per-path RECEIVE goroutines (scheduled by the very traffic that must trigger failover), so DOWN-detection no longer waits on a starved timer; uses TryLock (NOT Lock) to preserve Close's 'a reader never blocks on m.mu' invariant (Close holds m.mu while waiting on readersWG — a Lock would deadlock); ticks OUTSIDE the lock. Monotone-safe: Tick only marks an UP path DOWN once silence STRICTLY exceeds DownAfter and never brings a path UP, so a more frequent tick only lands a GENUINE DOWN sooner, never a false one; failback hysteresis untouched. Plus a modest timing tighten (1500/250 -> 1200/200ms) keeping the identical 6:1 six-lost-echo false-down tolerance. D16 reconciled: device.go + thresholds.go now read one telemetry.Default* source of truth, and the composition analysis budgets BOTH directions (max(edge,conc)). New sound TestP1Failover (failover_test.go) measures per-direction recovery from each daemon's scheduler-transition log timestamp (sub-ms, un-confounded — an ICMP-gap probe is unusable: it shares the netem queue with the saturating flow and measures congestion, not failover) + a data-plane iperf-bidir survival cross-check, strict < P1RecoverySeconds. R1: fable disapprove (the new concurrent sweep had NO -race unit coverage — no-op in bind unit tests since sweepIntervalNanos==0 unless StartProbeLoop runs; exercised only by the non-race e2e; acceptance requires -race unit tests) + filed D17 (TestPSKMismatchRejected flake); opus disapprove (thresholds D16 budget prose ~1.4s contradicted the 1.6s constant). R2 BOTH approve (concurrency crux -race-clean, Prober.mu serializes Liveness, no lock-order inversion; prose consistent).     fable (validation-soundness): widened flapFailoverPoll is observation-only; 3s per-cycle budget still asserted (failover_test.go:286) from daemon scheduler-transition timestamps (real latency); measured 1027-1364ms at the eager-detect floor discriminates fixed-from-masked. Failback asserted per cycle both ends. Load 0.6-1.9 >= the 0.55 repro point. Re-ran build/vet/fmt/lint/test -race green.
    
    HARDWARE (llm-ubuntu-0, amd64, bidirectional saturating load): flap 22/22 PASS (0 wedged, 0 budget-exceeded, max 1364ms vs 3000ms); single-kill 10/10 PASS (1135-1320ms, all < 1600ms failover budget). Resolves D18 + D21; completes the T20 flap acceptance.

# wanbond addressing oneshot (templated; instance = role: edge | concentrator).
# Re-applies interface addressing + link-up + policy rules + per-table routes
# + nft SNAT from an operator-owned script after wanbond-<role>.service
# (re)starts. Needed on hosts that do NOT use systemd-networkd for wanbond0
# (systemd-networkd watches the interface itself and needs no such recipe —
# see docs/install.md §4).
#
# Install:
#   cp wanbond-addressing@.service /etc/systemd/system/
#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh   # your re-apply logic, chmod +x
#   $EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.env  # optional EnvironmentFile
#   systemctl daemon-reload
#   systemctl enable --now wanbond-addressing@<role>.service
#
# DO NOT replace the ExecStartPre wait loop below with a plain ExecStartPost=
# on wanbond-<role>.service itself: that unit is Type=exec, so systemd marks
# it "started" the instant execve() returns -- BEFORE the daemon has created
# wanbond0 -- so an ExecStartPost there races tun creation, fails with
# "Cannot find device", and (being un-prefixed) fails the unit and trips
# Restart=on-failure. This is the exact race R27 found and fixed for the
# systemd-networkd recipe; see docs/install.md §4. This unit instead orders
# itself After= the daemon unit AND actively polls for the interface to
# exist before running, so it never races tun creation regardless of how
# long the daemon takes to create it.
#
# See docs/install.md §4 for the full persistence recipe and the
# tun_persist cross-link (this oneshot becomes optional-but-harmless once
# tun_persist is enabled, but stays useful as a first-boot/manual-teardown
# safety net).

[Unit]
Description=wanbond %i interface addressing re-apply (operator-owned, oneshot)
PartOf=wanbond-%i.service
After=wanbond-%i.service
ConditionPathExists=/etc/wanbond/addressing-%i.sh

[Service]
Type=oneshot
RemainAfterExit=yes
EnvironmentFile=-/etc/wanbond/addressing-%i.env
# Poll for wanbond0 rather than trusting unit-start ordering alone: the
# daemon exposes no sd_notify readiness, and wanbond-%i.service (Type=exec)
# is considered "started" long before it creates the interface (R27).
ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c 'i=0; while [ ! -e /sys/class/net/wanbond0 ]; do i=$((i+1)); [ "$i" -ge 60 ] && { echo "wanbond0 did not appear within 30s" >&2; exit 1; }; sleep 0.5; done'
ExecStart=/etc/wanbond/addressing-%i.sh
TimeoutStartSec=45s

[Install]
WantedBy=wanbond-%i.service

content.content:
720	#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)
721	
722	By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
723	the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
724	it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
725	dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
726	any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.
727	
728	Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
729	issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
730	stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
731	device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
732	referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to
733	`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to
734	being torn down on stop.
735	
736	> **NetworkManager (D39):** a *persistent* `wanbond0` still needs the
737	> NetworkManager `unmanaged-devices` drop-in on NM-managed hosts. Persistence
738	> keeps the link alive across restarts; it does **not** exempt the interface
739	> from NetworkManager, which would otherwise flush the addresses/routes the
740	> operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of

file.content:
#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)

By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.

Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to
`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to
being torn down on stop.

> **NetworkManager (D39):** a *persistent* `wanbond0` still needs the
> NetworkManager `unmanaged-devices` drop-in on NM-managed hosts. Persistence
> keeps the link alive across restarts; it does **not** exempt the interface
> from NetworkManager, which would otherwise flush the addresses/routes the
> operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of
> `tun_persist`.

## 5. Firewall

#### Persistence recipe for non-networkd hosts (`wanbond-addressing@.service`)

The `systemd-networkd` `.network` recipe above needs `systemd-networkd`
itself watching `wanbond0`. On a host that addresses the interface another
way — NetworkManager with the unmanaged-devices drop-in above but no
networkd `.network` file, or neither daemon running — use the shipped
templated oneshot instead, `packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service`
(instance = role: `edge` or `concentrator`):

```sh
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service /etc/systemd/system/
# Write your own re-apply script (address + link-up + policy rules +
# per-table routes + nft SNAT — whatever the role needs) and, optionally, an
# environment file it can read:
$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh    # or addressing-concentrator.sh
chmod +x /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh
$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.env   # optional EnvironmentFile
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now wanbond-addressing@edge.service   # instance = role
```

`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` is `PartOf=`/`After=wanbond-<role>.service`
(stops with the daemon, only ever starts after it) and
`ConditionPathExists=`s your `/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh` script — leave
it unwritten and the unit is skipped, not failed. It is a `Type=oneshot` with
`RemainAfterExit=yes` that runs `ExecStart=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh`,
optionally fed by `EnvironmentFile=-/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.env`.

**Do not replace its `ExecStartPre` wait loop with a plain `ExecStartPost=` on
`wanbond-<role>.service` itself.** That is the exact race R27 found and fixed:
`wanbond-edge.service`/`wanbond-concentrator.service` are `Type=exec`, so
systemd considers them started the instant `execve()` returns — **before**
the daemon has created `wanbond0` — so an `ExecStartPost` there runs against a
not-yet-existent interface, fails with "Cannot find device", and (being
un-prefixed) fails the unit and trips the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop
(the same failure mode as the raw `ip address add` `ExecStartPost` warned
against above). `wanbond-addressing@.service` avoids this by ordering itself
`After=` the daemon unit **and** actively polling `/sys/class/net/wanbond0` in
its own `ExecStartPre` until the interface exists, so it never races tun
creation regardless of how long the daemon takes to create it.

Once `tun_persist = true` (above) is enabled, `wanbond0` and the
addresses/routes/rules attached to it survive a daemon restart on their own,
so re-running this oneshot on every restart becomes **optional but
harmless** — keep it enabled anyway as a safety net for what `tun_persist`
does not cover (first boot, a manual `ip link delete wanbond0`, a host that
cannot enable `tun_persist`).

## 5. Firewall

file.content:
## 4. systemd units

Unit files live in `packaging/systemd/`:

```sh
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-edge.service /etc/systemd/system/          # edge box
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-concentrator.service /etc/systemd/system/  # VPS
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
```

- `systemctl reload wanbond-<role>` sends SIGHUP: the daemon re-reads the
  config and applies the **path diff** (add/remove uplinks) without tearing
  down the live tunnel. A bad reload is logged and the running config kept.
- `Restart=on-failure` restarts the daemon if the tunnel engine tears down
  unexpectedly (the process exits non-zero in that case by design).
- The units run with `CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_ADMIN` (TUN creation) plus
  standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
  `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.

diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index fd10693..98fa0ad 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -740,6 +740,54 @@ being torn down on stop.
 > operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of
 > `tun_persist`.
 
+#### Persistence recipe for non-networkd hosts (`wanbond-addressing@.service`)
+
+The `systemd-networkd` `.network` recipe above needs `systemd-networkd`
+itself watching `wanbond0`. On a host that addresses the interface another
+way — NetworkManager with the unmanaged-devices drop-in above but no
+networkd `.network` file, or neither daemon running — use the shipped
+templated oneshot instead, `packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service`
+(instance = role: `edge` or `concentrator`):
+
+```sh
+cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service /etc/systemd/system/
+# Write your own re-apply script (address + link-up + policy rules +
+# per-table routes + nft SNAT — whatever the role needs) and, optionally, an
+# environment file it can read:
+$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh    # or addressing-concentrator.sh
+chmod +x /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh
+$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.env   # optional EnvironmentFile
+systemctl daemon-reload
+systemctl enable --now wanbond-addressing@edge.service   # instance = role
+```
+
+`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` is `PartOf=`/`After=wanbond-<role>.service`
+(stops with the daemon, only ever starts after it) and has
+`ConditionPathExists=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh` — leave that script
+unwritten and the unit is skipped, not failed. It is a `Type=oneshot` with
+`RemainAfterExit=yes` that runs `ExecStart=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh`,
+optionally fed by `EnvironmentFile=-/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.env`.
+
+**Do not replace its `ExecStartPre` wait loop with a plain `ExecStartPost=` on
+`wanbond-<role>.service` itself.** That is the exact race R27 found and fixed:
+`wanbond-edge.service`/`wanbond-concentrator.service` are `Type=exec`, so
+systemd considers them started the instant `execve()` returns — **before**
+the daemon has created `wanbond0` — so an `ExecStartPost` there runs against a
+not-yet-existent interface, fails with "Cannot find device", and (being
+un-prefixed) fails the unit and trips the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop
+(the same failure mode as the raw `ip address add` `ExecStartPost` warned
+against above). `wanbond-addressing@.service` avoids this by ordering itself
+`After=` the daemon unit **and** actively polling `/sys/class/net/wanbond0` in
+its own `ExecStartPre` until the interface exists, so it never races tun
+creation regardless of how long the daemon takes to create it.
+
+Once `tun_persist = true` (above) is enabled, `wanbond0` and the
+addresses/routes/rules attached to it survive a daemon restart on their own,
+so re-running this oneshot on every restart becomes **optional but
+harmless** — keep it enabled anyway as a safety net for what `tun_persist`
+does not cover (first boot, a manual `ip link delete wanbond0`, a host that
+cannot enable `tun_persist`).
+
 ## 5. Firewall
 
 ### Concentrator: UDP listen port

Green, clean tree, git-dirty warning gone.

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented task T111 in worktree `implement/T111` (created off base 356d850, since the pre-existing worktree HEAD was stale at 60138a6): shipped `packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service`, a templated oneshot (instance = role) that re-applies operator-owned addressing/routing/nft SNAT after `wanbond-<role>.service` (re)starts, ordering itself after wanbond0's actual existence (`ExecStartPre` polling `/sys/class/net/wanbond0`) rather than trusting `After=` alone under `Type=exec` — the R27 lesson. Added the coupled C4 doc section to `docs/install.md` §4 and extended `internal/config/packaging_test.go` with a presence/shape test.
- **Achieved:** pass; `resultCommit` f3a59f81f5c252b03ce5df429ea9228c86c565d5 on branch `implement/T111`; `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` all green.
- **Discovered:** `systemd-analyze verify` is available in the nix dev shell but this sandbox has no root and a read-only `/etc`, so it cannot place `/usr/local/bin/wanbond` or `/etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh` — even the pre-existing `wanbond-edge.service`/`wanbond-concentrator.service` fail verify here for the identical reason (missing `/usr/local/bin/wanbond`, missing `/bin/kill`). I worked around this by running `systemd-analyze verify --instance=edge` against a copy of the unit with `ExecStart`/`ConditionPathExists`/`EnvironmentFile` paths substituted to point at real, executable stub files elsewhere on disk — this exercises 100% of the unit's own syntax/ordering/directives (`PartOf=`, `After=`, `ConditionPathExists=`, `Type=oneshot`, `RemainAfterExit=yes`, `ExecStartPre=`, `TimeoutStartSec=`, `[Install]`) and passed with exit 0, isolating the real-path failure to missing host artifacts, not a unit defect — the same "host-path artifacts" caveat R27's review already noted for the original two units.
- **Issues:** none blocking. `golangci-lint run ./...` surfaces 3 pre-existing issues in `internal/dnsresolve/{doh,dot}.go` and `internal/bind/pathsock.go` (errcheck/staticcheck) — unrelated to this task's files and not part of T111's required gate (`go build/vet/test`); left untouched per scope discipline.
